pattern: Walking an array from the tail is the same as popping a stack, so tracking which required values have been seen turns the process into a single linear scan that stops the moment the target set is complete.
hints: Each operation removes whatever is currently at the very end of the array, so simulate that by scanning the array from its last index backward.
Keep a set of the values you still need (1 through k) that you have already seen while scanning backward; you are done the instant every one of those values has appeared.
The answer is simply how many elements you scanned (i.e. how far from the end you had to go) at the moment your collected set first reaches size k — you never need to look further once that happens.

edge_inputs.description: the only needed value sits right at the end
the needed value sits at the very front, forcing a full pass
k equals the array length, every element is required
duplicates of an already-found value must not shorten the count
minimum possible array, single element equal to k
moderately long reversed run where each needed value appears exactly once, in increasing distance from the end
needed small values are buried under a long run of irrelevant large filler values
the last-needed value sits at the very front while duplicates of earlier-needed values appear repeatedly in between

stress.description: random arrays constructed so that 1..k are guaranteed present, padded with random filler and shuffled
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    nums = list(range(1, k + 1))
    while len(nums) < n:
        nums.append(rng.randint(1, n))
    rng.shuffle(nums)
    return [nums, k]
